questions.text: 'Generally speaking, this principle (i.e. that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members) and the statutory rules of company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties' - Re Frederick Inns Ltd [1991] ILRM 582 (HC) at 587 (Lardner J). You are required to discuss this application of the principle of separate corporate personality in the [specific context of holding companies and subsidiaries]{.underline}, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: 'In addition to consolidating the law, The Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish Company Law'.
questions.text: 'In addition to consolidating the law, The Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish Company Law'. You are required to identify [and]{.underline} discuss the implications of what you view to be the five main reforms introduced by the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case law and statute law in support of your views.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4All Ltd (the 'company') which operates a car… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4All Ltd (the 'company') which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the company which is a family run consortium headed up by Simon although Paul has worked in the company for 10 years. He worked first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. Simon, the majority shareholder And Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been 'double-jobbing' for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife Jackie. Specifically, Simon has heard that Jackie has been buying cars from Paul that were traded in to the company at a discounted price before selling them on for a significant profit on their website; and Paul has been using the Company's garage-workshop to service cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage workshop was closed. You are required to advise Simon on the following: a) Whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its Director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014 and b) The remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breaches of such duties. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case law and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: Foreign Holidays Ltd (the 'company') has operated as a travel agency… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Foreign Holidays Ltd (the 'company') has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland for a number of years. Business was very badly affected by the volcanic activity there which disrupted travel not only in Iceland but also across Europe. Sean and Seamus, the Company's directors and 50:50 shareholders decided to diversify and last year invested in new offerings -- cruises to the Mediterranean and city breaks in Paris. Having established contacts and been on a number of fact-finding cruises and visits to Paris, Seam and Seamus launched their new offerings in October last and had a great many enquiries from people who were interested in making bookings. In addition, in the last number of weeks a significant number of bookings to both destinations were made for mid 2016. The timing of the launch of the new offerings could not have been worse because a cruise ship sank over Christmas and on top of that Paris was the subject of a terrorist attack. These events prompted large scale cancellations -- permitted under the Company's contracts with customers of done 3 months before the cruise. Sean and Seamus have reviewed the Company's books of account and see that the Company's books of account and see that the Company's financial position is hopeless. The company has begun to let go all 10 employees as it could not continue paying their wages and to make ends meet, has even dipped into the PAYE/PRSI it retained from its employees instead of remitting it to the revenue commissioners. The rent is also overdue on the Company's premises and cruise companies and other suppliers are now agitating for payment for bookings made by th4e Company which will not now be fulfilled on account of the cancellations by its customers. Ascertaining the true position of the Company's debtors and creditors has not been made any easier by the fact that a fire destroyed a large number of its records and Sean and Seamus have to rely for the most part on memory and the organisers on their smart phones to reconstruct the company's records. Sean and Seamus estimated that the company's debts exceed its assets by some €2 million and took the decision to liquidate. Having placed the company into creditor's voluntary winding up and having appointed a liquidator, Sean and Seamus are so disgusted that they are thinking of emigrating from Ireland without waiting around any longer. You are required to advise Sean and Seamus on the possibility that they may be the subject of an application for restriction under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 and in particular the defences that will be available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Martin, an official working for Big Bank… Company, Ultra Vires.
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Martin, an official working for Big Bank Plc (the 'bank') which has been asked to make loan facilities available to two companies in the following circumstances: a) Tony's Timber Ltd (the Ltd company) was an existing private company which, following the commencement of the Companies Act 2014 converted to a 'private limited company by shares' governed by Parts 1 to 14 of the Act and adopted a new single document constitution which adopted the optional provisions in that Act. It wishes to borrow €1,500,000 from the Bank. The purpose of the loan is to enable the ltd Company acquire a farm. Prior to its conversion, the LTD Company had operated a timber mill which was its main object but of course now, it no longer has an objects clause. b) Jill's Fashions Public Limited Company (the PLC Company) also wishes to borrow €5,000,000. Its main object in its objects clause is the manufacture of ladies' fashion clothing. It however, now wishes to borrow the money to buy a factory that manufactures dog food. Martin specifically seeks your advice in relation to the [Capacity]{.underline} (i.e. [vires]{.underline}) of the Ltd Company and of the PLC Company to borrow the money from the bank for the stated purposes. You are also specifically required to advise on the extent to which, if any, the bank should enquire as to whether the lending is ultra vires the Ltd Company and the PLC Company. In answering this question, you are required to cite all relevant case law and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: 'Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as…
questions.text: 'Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance'. You are required to discuss this statement and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: 'The Companies Act 2014 makes three very significant changes to the registration of company charges: first, in relation…
questions.text: 'The Companies Act 2014 makes three very significant changes to the registration of company charges: first, in relation to the definition of charges that are required to be registered under s409 of the Act, secondly by providing for an optional two stage registration process; and thirdly, be legislating for the priority of charges created by companies'. You are required to [discuss]{.underline} this statement citing all case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Ltd (the Company) which operates a pet shop in Naas…
questions.text: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Ltd (the Company) which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company's business has been going downhill and Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: (a) He removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; (b) He caused the company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month, the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to two dispositions identified in both circumstances. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish company law."…
questions.text: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish company law." You are required to identify and discuss the implications of what you view to be the five main reforms introduced by the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your views.

questions.description: "Although there is no longer a Table A' included in the Companies Act 2014 it has been replaced by the concept of…
questions.text: "Although there is no longer a Table A' included in the Companies Act 2014 it has been replaced by the concept of 'optional provisions'. For most companies, this means that the contents of their new default constitution will not be radically different from that provided by Table A". You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air… Fiduciary, Company.
questions.text: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the "Company") which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The Company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from a company owned 50:50 by David and his brother Bart, called DB Limited, which has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise Joan and David on the proposed sale (or lease) of the mountain to the Company from DB Limited, having regard to the following: (A) sections 231, 238 and 239 of the Companies Act 2014; and (B) the fiduciary duties as directors now contained in section 228 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law. You must distinguish your answers to parts (a) and (b) of the question, both of which carry equal marks.

questions.description: In what circumstances will a floating charge crystallise?
questions.text: In what circumstances will a floating charge crystallise? In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their gate mother Madge. They received their shares from Madge under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy let the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were woNh. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's constitution, you see that 95(1) applies: (a) the directors of a company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any share; (b) the directors' power to decline to register a transfer of shares (other than on account of a matter specified in subsection (2)) shall cease to be exercisable on the expiry of 2 months after the date of delivery to the company of the instrument of transfer of the share." You are required to advise David and Deborah on their lights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in Lunch Time… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in Lunch Time Treats Limited (the "Company") which is in the business of supplying fresh pre-packed sandwiches, rolls, salads and other foodstuffs to local shops that do not have the facilities for making-up their oven produce. The Company's business is holding up reasonably well, but their greatest headache is in getting paid for goods supplied. The directors decide that they are going to take a harder line with the shops to which they supply food. It is decided that where payment of a sum in excess of €15,000 is overdue for 6 months, the Company will issue such shops (that are themselves limited companies) with a 21-day letter demanding payment, and in the event of non-compliance, the Company will present a petition to have the debtor company wound up on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to advise Richard and Judy on the law relating to the winding up of companies on grounds of inability to pay their debts. You are required to identify possible issues that might arise in the context of the facts provided. You are required to [discuss]{.underline} this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Business is good for Harry Peckem, an accountant with an insolvency… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Business is good for Harry Peckem, an accountant with an insolvency practice. Recently, Big Bank plc (the "Bank") appointed him receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the "Company") on foot of a debenture it has given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a premises in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The premises was acquired by the Company for €2,500,000 in 2007, at the height of the boom. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months if the economy continues to recover. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Hany is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank, and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver, having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company, and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in tum recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down tum until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross referring clients very successfully.
